section.title: Rejection at Nazareth and Ministry in Capernaum
section.sectionRationale: Luke opens Jesus' public ministry with a scene that serves as the keynote for the whole Gospel. In His hometown synagogue Jesus reads Isaiah 61 and announces that its good news to the poor and release to the captive is fulfilled in Him, then warns, by the examples of Elijah and Elisha, that God's grace reaches outsiders. The town's admiration turns to murderous rage. Jesus goes on to Capernaum, where His word carries authority over demons and disease, and He presses on to preach the kingdom because that is why He was sent.
section.thesis: Jesus announces Himself as the Spirit-anointed Servant who brings good news to the poor and freedom to the captive, and though His own town rejects Him for extending God's grace beyond its borders, His word proves to be a word of authority that frees, heals, and cannot be confined.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the return to Galilee in the Spirit's power; the Nazareth manifesto from Isaiah 61; the offense of grace to outsiders and the town's rage; the word of authority over an unclean spirit at Capernaum; and healings at Simon's house and the mission to preach the kingdom everywhere.

units.label: Return in the Spirit's power
units.note: teaching in the synagogues of Galilee

verses.text: Jesus returned to Galilee in the power of the Spirit, and the news about Him spread throughout the surrounding region.
He taught in their synagogues and was glorified by everyone.

units.label: The Nazareth manifesto
units.note: the Spirit of the Lord is on Me

verses.text: Then Jesus came to Nazareth, where He had been brought up. As was His custom, He entered the synagogue on the Sabbath. And when He stood up to read,
the scroll of the prophet Isaiah was handed to Him. Unrolling it, He found the place where it was written:

“The Spirit of the Lord is on Me, because He has anointed Me to preach {{euangelizo|good news}} to the poor. He has sent Me to proclaim {{aphesis|liberty}} to the captives and recovery of sight to the blind, to release the oppressed,
to proclaim the year of the Lord’s favor.”
Then He rolled up the scroll, returned it to the attendant, and sat down. The eyes of everyone in the synagogue were fixed on Him,
and He began by saying, “Today this Scripture is fulfilled in your hearing.”

units.label: The offense of grace to outsiders
units.note: no prophet is accepted in his hometown

verses.text: All spoke well of Him and marveled at the gracious words that came from His lips. “Isn’t this the son of Joseph?” they asked.
Jesus said to them, “Surely you will quote this proverb to Me: ‘Physician, heal yourself! Do here in Your hometown what we have heard that You did in Capernaum.’”
Then He added, “Truly I tell you, no prophet is {{dektos|accepted}} in his hometown.
But I tell you truthfully that there were many widows in Israel in the time of Elijah, when the sky was shut for three and a half years and great famine swept over all the land.
Yet Elijah was not sent to any of them, but to the widow of Zarephath in Sidon.
And there were many lepers in Israel in the time of Elisha the prophet. Yet not one of them was cleansed, only Naaman the Syrian.”
On hearing this, all the people in the synagogue were enraged.
They got up, drove Him out of the town, and led Him to the brow of the hill on which the town was built, in order to throw Him over the cliff.
But Jesus passed through the crowd and went on His way.

units.label: A word of authority at Capernaum
units.note: with authority He commands the unclean spirits

verses.text: Then He went down to Capernaum, a town in Galilee, and on the Sabbath He began to teach the people.
They were astonished at His teaching, because His message had {{exousia|authority}}.
In the synagogue there was a man possessed by the spirit of an unclean demon. He cried out in a loud voice,
“Ha! What do You want with us, Jesus of Nazareth? Have You come to destroy us? I know who You are, the Holy One of God!”
But Jesus rebuked the demon. “Be silent!” He said. “Come out of him!” At this, the demon threw the man down before them all and came out without harming him.
All the people were overcome with amazement and asked one another, “What is this message? With authority and power He commands the unclean spirits, and they come out!”
And the news about Jesus spread throughout the surrounding region.

units.label: Healings and the mission to preach
units.note: I must preach the good news of the kingdom

verses.text: After Jesus had left the synagogue, He went to the home of Simon, whose mother-in-law was suffering from a high fever. So they appealed to Jesus on her behalf,
and He stood over her and rebuked the fever, and it left her. And she got up at once and began to serve them.
At sunset, all who were ill with various diseases were brought to Jesus, and laying His hands on each one, He healed them.
Demons also came out of many people, shouting, “You are the Son of God!” But He rebuked the demons and would not allow them to speak, because they knew He was the Christ.
At daybreak, Jesus went out to a solitary place, and the crowds were looking for Him. They came to Him and tried to keep Him from leaving.
But Jesus told them, “I must preach the good news of the kingdom of God to the other towns as well, because that is why I was sent.”
And He continued to preach in the synagogues of Judea.

euangelizo.gloss: To announce good news, to proclaim glad tidings. In the Isaiah text Jesus reads, the Anointed One is sent to euangelizo the poor. Luke loves this word; here it names the very heart of Jesus' mission, a gospel first for the humble, the broken, and the outsider.

aphesis.gloss: Release, liberty, forgiveness. The word for freeing prisoners is the same word used for the forgiveness of sins. Isaiah's promise of liberty to captives points beyond political freedom to the deeper release Jesus brings, freedom from sin, guilt, and bondage to evil.

exousia.gloss: Authority, the rightful power to act. The crowds are astonished that Jesus' word carries exousia: He teaches with it and commands unclean spirits with it, and they obey. His authority is not borrowed from the scribes but is His own as the Holy One of God.

dektos.gloss: Acceptable, welcome, favorable. Jesus proclaims the acceptable year of the Lord, the time of God's favor, and then says no prophet is dektos, accepted, in his hometown. The same word frames both the offer of grace and the tragedy of its rejection.

basic.context: Jesus begins His public work in Galilee, teaching in synagogues with growing fame. In Nazareth, His hometown, He reads from Isaiah 61 about the Spirit-anointed Servant who brings good news to the poor and freedom to the captive, and declares that this is fulfilled in Him. When He points out that God once sent Elijah and Elisha to help outsiders rather than Israelites, the crowd turns violent. Jesus moves on to Capernaum, where His word drives out a demon and heals the sick, and He insists on preaching the kingdom everywhere.
basic.hermeneutics: The Nazareth scene is programmatic: Luke places it first to announce the themes of the whole Gospel, good news to the poor, release to captives, and grace that overflows to outsiders. Read the Elijah and Elisha examples as the reason for the rage; the offense is not merely a local prophet but a gospel that reaches beyond the boundaries of the insider. The Capernaum scenes then show the announced liberty happening in real deliverance and healing.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus is anointed to euangelizo, to bring good news, to the poor, and to proclaim aphesis, release, to captives, the same word used for the forgiveness of sins. He proclaims the dektos, acceptable, year of the Lord, then notes that no prophet is dektos, accepted, at home. In Capernaum His word carries exousia, authority, so that even unclean spirits obey.
basic.typology: Jesus fulfills Isaiah's Servant who preaches the year of the Lord's favor, the true Jubilee. Elijah's mercy to the widow of Zarephath and Elisha's cleansing of Naaman the Syrian foreshadow the gospel reaching the nations. The synagogue rejection anticipates the greater rejection at Jerusalem, and Jesus passing through the murderous crowd foreshadows that His hour is not yet.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Spirit-anointed Messiah of Isaiah 61, the one in whom the year of God's favor dawns. He is the Holy One of God whose word commands demons and disease. He deliberately extends grace beyond the insider and is rejected for it, a rejection that foreshadows the cross, yet He cannot be stopped and presses on to preach the kingdom.
basic.god: God's ancient promise of a Jubilee of release comes true in Jesus. God's grace is shown to be free and sovereign, reaching a Sidonian widow and a Syrian general, not bound by nation or merit. God's word in Jesus carries real authority to free and heal, and God's purpose sends Jesus onward, for that is why He was sent.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 61:1-2 is the text Jesus reads and fulfills. 1 Kings 17 and 2 Kings 5 tell of Elijah and Elisha and the outsiders they blessed. Luke 7:22 echoes the manifesto in Jesus' answer to John. Acts shows the gospel going out to the nations, fulfilling the pattern set here.
basic.oneStory: The year of the Lord's favor has arrived in the person of Jesus, who brings the poor good news and the captive release. His own town rejects the grace that overflows to outsiders, foreshadowing the cross, yet the word of the kingdom will not be silenced. It goes out with authority to free and heal, running ahead toward the nations, exactly as Isaiah promised and Acts will record.

context.literary: Luke moves the Nazareth rejection to the front of Jesus' ministry, ahead of much that Mark and Matthew record first, precisely because it announces his Gospel's themes. The reading of Isaiah 61, the sermon's turn to Elijah and Elisha, the town's rage, and then the Capernaum deliverance and healings form a diptych: the manifesto declared, then the manifesto enacted. The closing statement about preaching the kingdom sets the pattern for the ministry that follows.
context.historical: Synagogue worship included readings from the Law and the Prophets, and a visiting teacher might be invited to read and comment, standing to read and sitting to teach. Nazareth was a small, unremarkable town, which sharpens the question, Is this not Joseph's son? Zarephath lay in Gentile Sidon and Naaman was a Syrian, so the examples deliberately name outsiders, and to a proud audience that was intolerable.
context.canonical: Isaiah 61 describes the Spirit-anointed herald of the Lord's favor, and Jesus claims to be its fulfillment. The overflow of grace to Zarephath and Naaman prefigures the Gentile mission of Acts. The authority of Jesus' word over demons and disease demonstrates the in-breaking kingdom of God, a central theme that Luke will develop from here to the cross and resurrection.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To set out the program of Jesus' ministry through the Nazareth manifesto, to show that the grace He brings reaches the poor and the outsider and is therefore offensive to the self-assured, and to demonstrate through the Capernaum deliverance and healings that His word carries the real authority of the dawning kingdom.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes particular events, a synagogue reading, a rejection, specific healings, but it prescribes the enduring shape of the gospel: good news for the poor, release for captives, and grace that will not be confined to insiders. The reaction at Nazareth warns every generation against presuming on nearness to Jesus while resisting the reach of His grace.

debates.question: Why did the crowd turn from admiration to murderous rage so quickly?

views.label: Offense at grace going to outsiders
views.case: Their anger flares precisely when Jesus cites Elijah and Elisha helping a Sidonian widow and a Syrian leper; the thought that God's favor would bypass insiders and reach Gentiles was intolerable to them.

views.label: Wounded local pride and demand for signs
views.case: They wanted the hometown boy to perform in Nazareth what He had done in Capernaum; when He refused to be their private wonder-worker and exposed their unbelief, offended pride turned to fury.

debates.bearing: Both readings expose the same heart: a claim on Jesus that resents His freedom to give grace as He wills, a warning against presuming on privilege while resisting God's generosity.

debates.question: What is the year of the Lord's favor that Jesus proclaims?

views.label: The fulfillment of Jubilee
views.case: Isaiah drew on the year of Jubilee, Leviticus 25, when debts were released and captives freed; Jesus announces that the true, ultimate release, forgiveness and freedom from sin, has arrived in Him.

views.label: The dawning of the messianic age of salvation
views.case: The acceptable year names the whole era of salvation now breaking in, the time of God's favor when the promises are being fulfilled and the kingdom is drawing near.

debates.bearing: Either way, Jesus declares that the long-awaited time of release and favor is not future but present in Him, today, for all who will receive it.

study.originalLanguages: The manifesto turns on rich words. Jesus is anointed to euangelizo, to announce good news, to the poor, and to proclaim aphesis, release, to captives, the same term Luke uses for the forgiveness of sins, so that the liberty promised runs deeper than politics to the freeing of guilty hearts. He proclaims the dektos, the acceptable or favorable, year of the Lord, then observes with sorrow that no prophet is dektos, accepted, in his hometown, the same word framing both the offer and its refusal. In Capernaum His teaching and His command over the unclean spirit carry exousia, genuine authority, so that the announced release is not only spoken but enacted before their eyes.

typology.fulfillment: Jesus proclaims and fulfills the true year of the Lord's favor, releasing captives from the deeper bondage of sin.

typology.type: Elijah sent to the widow of Zarephath in Sidon
typology.fulfillment: God's grace reaching an outsider foreshadows the gospel going to the Gentiles, fulfilled in Acts.
A Gentile leper cleansed by grace prefigures the nations welcomed and washed through Christ.

study.christ: Jesus stands in the synagogue and applies Isaiah 61 to Himself: He is the Spirit-anointed Servant in whom the year of the Lord's favor has come. He brings the poor good news and the captive release, and He is the Holy One of God whose bare word silences and expels demons and lifts fevered bodies. He knowingly extends grace beyond Israel and is rejected for it in His own town, a foretaste of the greater rejection to come, yet He passes untouched through the crowd because His hour has not yet arrived. Driven by His mission, He presses on to preach the kingdom, for that is why He was sent.
study.god: The God of Israel keeps His word: the Jubilee He promised through Isaiah dawns in Jesus. He is a God whose grace is gloriously free, reaching a foreign widow and an enemy general, unbound by nation, status, or merit, which is why proud insiders rage. His authority stands behind Jesus' word so that evil spirits and diseases obey, evidence that His kingdom is breaking in. And His purpose propels the mission outward, town after town, until the good news of the kingdom is preached to all.

crossReferences.note: The Spirit-anointed herald who brings good news to the poor and proclaims the year of the Lord's favor, the text Jesus reads and claims to fulfill.
Elijah sent to the widow of Zarephath in Sidon, an outsider shown God's mercy, cited by Jesus.
Elisha's cleansing of Naaman the Syrian, a Gentile healed by grace, cited by Jesus.
Jesus answers John's question by echoing the manifesto: the poor hear good news and the blind receive sight, the promised works fulfilled.

study.oneStory: In a small-town synagogue Jesus unrolls Isaiah and announces that the year of the Lord's favor has come in Him, good news for the poor and release for the captive. When He shows that this grace overflows to outsiders, His own townsfolk try to kill Him, a shadow of the cross that lies ahead. But the word will not be stopped: in Capernaum it frees the possessed and heals the sick with real authority, and Jesus drives onward to preach the kingdom, carrying the gospel toward the very nations Elijah and Elisha once served.

sermon.title: This Scripture Is Fulfilled
sermon.why: Preaching Luke 4:16-30, Begg shows Jesus proclaiming Himself the long-awaited Messiah in His hometown synagogue and the crowd's turn to rage, pressing the point that there are only two responses to Jesus, to bow before Him or to reject Him. A faithful, expository handling of the manifesto at the heart of this movement.

quiz.q: What did Jesus claim when He read from Isaiah in the Nazareth synagogue?

options.text: That the Spirit-anointed Servant who brings good news to the poor and release to captives was fulfilled in Him that very day.
options.note: In verse 21 Jesus says, Today this Scripture is fulfilled in your hearing, applying Isaiah 61 to Himself.

options.text: That the prophecy of Isaiah would one day be fulfilled by the Messiah.
options.note: True of Isaiah's promise, but Jesus' startling claim was that it was fulfilled today, in Him, not merely someday.

options.text: That He admired Isaiah as a great prophet of the past.
options.note: He did far more than admire Isaiah; He announced Himself as the fulfillment of the prophecy.

options.text: That the passage applied only to the nation's political freedom.
options.note: The release Jesus proclaims is deeper than politics, the liberty of forgiveness and the year of the Lord's favor.

quiz.q: Why did the people of Nazareth become enraged?

options.text: Because Jesus showed that God's grace, through Elijah and Elisha, reached outsiders like the widow of Zarephath and Naaman the Syrian.
options.note: Their fury flares in verses 25 to 28 when Jesus names Gentiles as recipients of God's mercy.

options.text: Because Jesus refused to perform miracles in His hometown as He had in Capernaum.
options.note: That demand is part of the friction, but the rage erupts specifically at the examples of grace to outsiders.

options.text: Because they could not believe the carpenter's son could read Scripture.
options.note: They marveled at His gracious words; it was the message of grace to outsiders, not His literacy, that enraged them.

options.text: Because Jesus openly denied being the Messiah.
options.note: The opposite: He announced that Isaiah's promise was fulfilled in Him.

quiz.q: What amazed the people of Capernaum about Jesus?

options.text: That His word carried such authority that even unclean spirits obeyed His command.
options.note: Verses 32 and 36 stress the authority of His teaching and His command over demons.

options.text: That He was a compelling and eloquent speaker.
options.note: His words were gracious, but Luke's point is the authority behind them, not mere eloquence.

options.text: That He performed tricks to entertain the crowd.
options.note: These were genuine deliverances and healings, not entertainment or tricks.

options.text: That He taught exactly like the other scribes.
options.note: The contrast is precisely that His teaching had an authority the scribes lacked.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus leave the crowds who wanted Him to stay in Capernaum?

options.text: Because He was sent to preach the good news of the kingdom of God to the other towns as well.
options.note: Verse 43 gives His reason: that is why He was sent.

options.text: Because He needed rest from the demands of the crowds.
options.note: He did withdraw to pray, but the stated reason for leaving was His mission to preach elsewhere.

options.text: Because the people of Capernaum had rejected Him.
options.note: They wanted Him to stay; He left not from rejection but for the sake of the wider mission.

options.text: Because His work of preaching was finished.
options.note: His work was only beginning; He left to extend it to other towns.

quiz.q: What does the reach of God's grace to Zarephath and Naaman reveal about Jesus' mission?

options.text: That the good news is for the poor and the outsider, not for insiders only, pointing ahead to the nations.
options.note: Jesus' examples announce a gospel that overflows every boundary, fulfilled as the church goes to the Gentiles.

options.text: That God sometimes helps foreigners as well as His own people.
options.note: True but understated; Jesus makes this the very pattern of His mission, not an occasional exception.

options.text: That Israel had been entirely replaced and cast off.
options.note: Jesus is Israel's Messiah offering grace to all; the point is inclusion of outsiders, not the rejection of Israel.

options.text: That grace must be earned by the deserving.
options.note: The widow and the Syrian were undeserving outsiders; the point is grace freely given, not earned.

questions: Jesus announced good news for the poor, the captive, the blind, and the oppressed. Where in your life do you most need to hear that His good news is for the weak and not only the strong?
The people of Nazareth loved Jesus until His grace reached beyond their borders. Is there a group of outsiders whose welcome into God's family you find hard to celebrate?
Jesus said, 'Today this Scripture is fulfilled in your hearing.' What would it mean to receive Him today rather than admiring Him at a safe distance?
The word of Jesus carried authority to free the possessed and heal the sick. Where do you need to trust that His word still has authority over what binds you?
Jesus pressed on to other towns because that is why He was sent. How does His sense of mission challenge your own sense of purpose and calling?

translationNotes.note: Jesus reads from Isaiah 61:1-2 in the Greek Septuagint, which speaks of recovery of sight to the blind. The BSB footnote notes that some manuscripts add to heal the brokenhearted after the poor, filling out the Isaiah text; Luke's reading still names the good news to the poor as central.

The year of the Lord's favor can also be rendered the acceptable year of the Lord. It draws on the year of Jubilee, Leviticus 25, when captives were freed and debts released, now fulfilled in the salvation Jesus brings. Jesus stops the reading before Isaiah's next line about the day of vengeance, leaving the accent on grace.

Leper and leprosy in Scripture cover a range of serious skin diseases, not only the condition called leprosy today, as the BSB footnote explains with reference to Leviticus 13. Naaman the Syrian was a Gentile military commander cleansed by grace, 2 Kings 5.

The BSB reads the synagogues of Judea, while some manuscripts read Galilee, as the footnote notes and as Mark 1:39 has. Luke often uses Judea broadly for the land of the Jews, so the wider reading may simply mean the Jewish homeland.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 4:14-44. Headings, line breaks, poem and note markers, and footnotes were skipped when assembling verse text. One em dash in verse 27 (cleansed, only Naaman) and one in verse 34 (You are, the Holy One) were converted to commas; no other dash characters remain.
